pending_evaluation.article:
**Choosing the Right Metric: Why Accuracy Alone Can Mislead You**

Imagine you build a model to detect fraudulent credit card transactions. Your dataset has 10,000 transactions, and only 100 of them are fraud. You train a model, and it achieves 99% accuracy. Impressive, right? Not quite. If the model simply labels every transaction as "not fraud," it still gets 99% accuracy — and catches zero fraudulent cases. This is the accuracy paradox, and it is one of the most common traps in applied machine learning.

The problem is that accuracy measures overall correctness, but it says nothing about *how* errors are distributed. In real business problems, not all errors cost the same. Missing a fraud case costs far more than flagging a legitimate transaction for review. This asymmetry is why data scientists use additional metrics to evaluate model performance.

**Precision and Recall**

Precision answers the question: "Of all the cases the model flagged as positive, how many were actually positive?" A high-precision model avoids false alarms. Recall answers a different question: "Of all the actual positive cases, how many did the model catch?" A high-recall model misses fewer real events.

In fraud detection, recall is usually more important. You want to catch as many fraudulent transactions as possible, even if that means reviewing some false alarms. In a spam filter, however, precision may matter more. Users get frustrated when legitimate emails are incorrectly blocked.

**The F1 Score and AUC-ROC**

When you need to balance precision and recall, the F1 score is useful. It is the harmonic mean of both metrics, so it penalizes extreme imbalance. A model with 90% precision and 10% recall will have a poor F1 score, even though each individual number may sound acceptable.

The AUC-ROC curve takes a broader view. It measures how well the model separates positive from negative cases across all possible classification thresholds. A perfect model scores 1.0. A random model scores 0.5. In practice, AUC-ROC is especially useful when you are comparing multiple models before choosing which one to deploy.

**Business Alignment Matters**

Selecting a metric is not only a technical decision. It reflects business priorities. A healthcare company screening for a rare disease will prioritize recall — missing a sick patient is unacceptable. A marketing team scoring sales leads may prioritize precision — they want quality, not volume.

Before building any model, experienced data scientists ask: "What does a wrong prediction actually cost?" The answer shapes every evaluation decision that follows. Metrics are not just numbers. They are business commitments.
pending_evaluation.writing_task:
Write **2–3 sentences** summarizing the main argument of the article.

Your sentences should answer these three questions:
1. What is the central problem with using accuracy alone?
2. What alternative metrics does the article introduce?
3. Why does the choice of metric matter beyond the technical level?

Write in simple, clear English. Use your own words — do not copy sentences directly from the article.
